prompt: During a pre-trip inspection, the air compressor governor typically cuts out (stops pumping) at about which pressure range?

options.text: Around 120–140 psi
Around 40–60 psi
Around 60–80 psi
Around 180–200 psi

explanation: Most air-brake systems cut out near 120–140 psi so the compressor does not over-pressurize the tanks. Drivers must know cut-in/cut-out ranges for safe operation.

distractorExplanations.b: 40–60 psi is far below normal operating pressure and would indicate a serious air-system problem.
distractorExplanations.c: 60–80 psi is closer to a low-air warning area, not the cut-out range. Learners who choose “Around 60–80 psi” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: 180–200 psi exceeds typical governor cut-out and could indicate a malfunctioning governor.

prompt: What is the primary purpose of a slack adjuster on an S-cam air brake?

options.text: To increase air tank capacity
To maintain proper brake-chamber pushrod travel / brake adjustment
To cool the brake drums
To filter moisture from the air lines

explanation: Slack adjusters convert chamber pushrod force into cam rotation and must be adjusted (manual or automatic) so pushrod travel stays within limits.

distractorExplanations.a: Air tank capacity is fixed by reservoir design, not slack adjusters. Learners who choose “To increase air tank capacity” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: Cooling is not the function of a slack adjuster. Learners who choose “To cool the brake drums” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: Air dryers and drain valves manage moisture, not slack adjusters. Learners who choose “To filter moisture from the air lines” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: If the low air pressure warning activates while driving, what should you do first?

options.text: Continue to the next exit at highway speed
Pump the brake pedal rapidly to rebuild pressure
Stop safely as soon as possible and park until the problem is fixed
Disable the warning light and finish the trip

explanation: Low-air warning means braking capability is compromised. Stop safely, secure the vehicle, and do not continue until air pressure is restored and the defect is corrected.

distractorExplanations.a: Continuing at highway speed risks brake failure. Learners who choose “Continue to the next exit at highway speed” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.b: Pumping the pedal wastes remaining air and is not a fix. Learners who choose “Pump the brake pedal rapidly to rebuild pressure” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: Ignoring a low-air warning is unsafe and illegal. Learners who choose “Disable the warning light and finish the trip” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: On a dual air brake system, why are there typically two separate air tanks/circuits?

options.text: To double fuel economy
To power only the trailer ABS
To eliminate the need for a parking brake
So one circuit can still provide braking if the other fails

explanation: Dual systems isolate front and rear (or primary/secondary) circuits so a leak in one does not leave the vehicle with zero service brakes.

distractorExplanations.a: Air circuits do not improve fuel economy. Learners who choose “To double fuel economy” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.b: ABS uses the air system but dual tanks are for redundancy, not ABS alone. Learners who choose “To power only the trailer ABS” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: Parking brakes (spring brakes) are still required. Learners who choose “To eliminate the need for a parking brake” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: What does an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) primarily help a driver do?

options.text: Maintain steering control during hard braking on slippery surfaces
Shorten stopping distance on dry pavement in all cases
Eliminate the need for following distance
Automatically downshift the transmission

explanation: ABS prevents wheel lockup so the driver can steer while braking hard; it does not guarantee shorter stops on every surface.

distractorExplanations.b: ABS may not shorten stops on dry pavement versus properly applied non-ABS brakes. Learners who choose “Shorten stopping distance on dry pavement in all cases” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: Following distance is still required with ABS. Learners who choose “Eliminate the need for following distance” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: ABS does not control transmission shifting. Learners who choose “Automatically downshift the transmission” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: During an en-route inspection of cargo, what is a key reason to check tire pressure when tires are cool if possible?

options.text: Cool tires always have zero pressure
Heat from driving raises pressure and can mask underinflation
CDL rules forbid checking hot tires
Tire pressure only matters for passenger cars

explanation: Heat increases gauge pressure; checking after long runs can hide underinflation. Underinflated tires overheat and can fail.

distractorExplanations.a: Cool tires still hold rated pressure when properly inflated. Learners who choose “Cool tires always have zero pressure” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: Hot tires can be checked cautiously; cool checks are preferred for accuracy. Learners who choose “CDL rules forbid checking hot tires” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: Commercial tires require proper inflation just as much as car tires. Learners who choose “Tire pressure only matters for passenger cars” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: What is the function of the trailer air supply (red) glad hand / supply line?

options.text: To provide only electrical power to lights
To steer the trailer axles
To charge the trailer air tanks and control the trailer emergency/parking brakes
To cool the trailer refrigeration unit

explanation: The supply (emergency) line charges trailer reservoirs and, if disconnected or loses pressure, applies trailer emergency brakes.

distractorExplanations.a: Electrical power uses the 7-way cable, not the red air line. Learners who choose “To provide only electrical power to lights” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.b: Steering is not controlled by glad hands. Learners who choose “To steer the trailer axles” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: Reefer units have their own power/fuel systems. Learners who choose “To cool the trailer refrigeration unit” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: A driver notices the water temperature gauge climbing into the danger zone. Which action is most appropriate?

options.text: Shut off the engine immediately while still in traffic
Increase speed to force more air through the radiator
Cover the radiator to trap heat
Pull off safely, idle to cool if safe, and investigate before continuing

explanation: Overheating requires a safe stop and diagnosis (coolant, belts, debris). Stopping in traffic lanes or forcing the engine harder worsens risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Immediate shutdown in a travel lane creates a collision hazard; get clear first when possible.
distractorExplanations.b: Higher speed increases load and may worsen overheating. Learners who choose “Increase speed to force more air through the radiator” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: Covering the radiator traps heat and damages the engine. Learners who choose “Cover the radiator to trap heat” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: What is a common sign of a leaking or defective shock absorber?

options.text: Excessive bouncing after hitting a bump
Increased fuel tank capacity
Higher governed RPM
Automatic parking brake application

explanation: Worn shocks allow prolonged bouncing and poor tire contact, reducing control and increasing wear The correct choice (“Excessive bouncing after hitting a bump”) is the one that matches this rule.

distractorExplanations.b: Shocks do not change fuel capacity. Learners who choose “Increased fuel tank capacity” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: Governed RPM is engine control, unrelated to shocks. Learners who choose “Higher governed RPM” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: Parking brakes are spring-brake systems, not shock-related. Learners who choose “Automatic parking brake application” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

options.text: It always reduces stopping distance

It lowers bridge formula weight
It raises the center of gravity and increases rollover risk in turns

explanation: High CG makes rollovers more likely at the same speed in curves; load low and centered when possible.

distractorExplanations.a: Top-heavy loads often worsen stopping/handling, not improve them. Learners who choose “It always reduces stopping distance” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.b: ABS cannot overcome physics of a high CG. Learners who choose “It improves ABS performance” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: Bridge formula concerns axle spacing/weight, not CG height directly. Learners who choose “It lowers bridge formula weight” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: When covering a load with a tarp, a key securement principle is:

options.text: Secure tarp edges so wind cannot get under and tear the cover or unload cargo
Leave one side completely open for ventilation always
Use the tarp instead of required tiedowns

explanation: Wind can balloon poorly secured tarps; covers must be fastened so they protect cargo without replacing required tiedowns.

prompt: What does 'working load limit' (WLL) of a tiedown refer to?

options.text: The weight of the empty trailer
The maximum load the device is designed to secure under normal use
The driver's maximum driving hours

explanation: WLL is used to ensure aggregate tiedown capacity meets cargo weight requirements under FMCSA securement rules.

distractorExplanations.a: Empty trailer weight is not WLL. Learners who choose “The weight of the empty trailer” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.c: HOS is unrelated to tiedown WLL. Learners who choose “The driver's maximum driving hours” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.
distractorExplanations.d: Tire ply is a separate rating. Learners who choose “The tire ply rating only” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: If cargo shifts while you are driving, what should you do?

Ignore it if the trailer doors are closed
Stop safely as soon as possible and resecure/rearrange before continuing
Brake hard from highway speed immediately in your lane

explanation: Shifted cargo changes handling and can spill; stop clear of traffic and fix securement The correct choice (“Stop safely as soon as possible and resecure/rearrange befo…”) is the one that matches this rule.

distractorExplanations.a: Higher speed can worsen instability. Learners who choose “Increase speed to stabilize the load” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

distractorExplanations.d: Panic braking risks jackknife/loss of control; get to a safe stop. Learners who choose “Brake hard from highway speed immediately in your lane” are usually making that exact mistake instead of applying the rule in the stem.

prompt: Bridge formula / axle spacing rules exist mainly to:
